content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

January 11, 1984

L. N. "Judge" Landis
Chairperson
Bay Area Air Quality Management District
939 Ellis Street
San Francisco, CA 94109 S3-79

Re: Advice Request No. A-84-005

Dear Mr. Landis:

You have asked whether a member of the Bay Area Air Quality
Management District ("BAAQMD") Board can have a conflict of
interest under the Political Reform Act with respect to the
adoption of an Air Quality Management Plan ("AQMP"). You have
enclosed a general description of an AQMP, as well the actual
AQMP adopted by BAAQMD in 1982.

The Clean Air Act amendments adopted by Congress in 1977
require states to develop plans to meet statutory air quality
standards by 1987. As part of this process, an AQMP must be
adopted for the Bay Area. The AQMP is developed and approved,
after public hearings, by BAAQMD, the Metropolitan Transportation Commission and the Association of Bay Area Governments.
The AQMP identifies measures to be adopted in order to achieve
the required air quality standards. The measures describe
sources of pollution, the types of emission, and the means of
reducing pollution, to be addressed by BAAQMD regulations.

The measures on the list for adoption are ranked by the
staff in order of their cost-effectiveness. Measures not
approved by the BAAQMD for adoption are put on contingency
status and are placed at the end of the list. A cut-off line is
drawn at that point on the list at which, if the pollution
reduction predictions for the measures are accurate, the
required air quality standards would be met. If the predictions
are accurate, it would be necessary to implement all the
measures above the cut-off line in order to meet the Federal
requirements by 1987; it would not be necessary to implement the

Mr. L. N. Landis
January 11, 1984
Page Two


measures below the line. A measure is not effective, however,
until BAAQMD adopts a specific regulation to implement the
measure. Thus, the AQMP does not directly regulate any company
or industry. It merely identifies the measures for which
regulations will be adopted.

ANALYSIS

The Political Reform Act requires a public official to
disqualify himself or herself from any governmental decision in
which the official knows or has reason to know that he or she
has a financial interest. Government Code Section 87100.1/
Section 87103 provides, in part, that:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
     within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
     foreseeable that the decision will have a material
     financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on
     the public generally, on:

          (a) Any business entity in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect investment worth
     more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

          (b) Any real property in which the public
     official has a direct or indirect investment worth
     more than one thousand dollars ($1,000).

          (c) Any source of income, other than loans
     by a commercial lending institution in the regular
     course of business on terms available to the
     public without regard to official status,
     aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or
     more in value provided to, received by or promised
     to the public official within 12 months prior to
     the time when the decision is made.

          (d) Any business entity in which the public
     official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
     employee, or holds any position of management.


1/ All statutory references made are to the Government
Code unless otherwise stated.

Mr. L. N. Landis
January 11, 1984
Page Three


     A member of the BAAQMD Board is required to disqualify
himself or herself from a decision on an AQMP if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, on a business entity or on real property, in which
the official has an investment of over $1,000, from which the
official has received income of over $250, or of which the
official is an officer or employee.

     You should note that the key to whether an official has a
conflict of interest is whether some specific effect of the AQMP
decision/ on the official's interests is "reasonably
foreseeable." The Commission has interpreted this term on
several occasions, holding that an effect is "reasonably
foreseeable" whenever there is a "substantial probability" that
it will occur. E.g., Opinion request by Tom Thorner, No.
75-089, 1 FPPC Opinions 198, 204-206 (1975). If the effect is
likely, but not certain, it may still be reasonably
foreseeable. The fact that intermediate steps, such as the
adoption of a regulation to implement a policy, must be taken,
does not necessarily mean that an effect is not reasonably
foreseeable.

     For example, the Commission has specifically ruled that
disqualification may be required as to a governmental decision
on the adoption of a city redevelopment plan,/ or a local
coastal plan,/ even though such plans can only be implemented
by future decisions, and can be amended at any time. Similarly,
disqualification has been required when one city council voted
on a proposal to recommend certain action to a second city
council; in that case, not only was the vote not on the actual
decision, but the first city council did not even have the
authority to implement its recommendation./ Of course, the
determination of reasonable foreseeability rests ultimately upon
the specific facts of each case.


_____________________

     2/ The vote on the AQMP is clearly a "governmental
decision." 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(D)(1) (copy
enclosed).

     3/ Opinion requested by Thomas Oglesby, No. 75-083,
1 FPPC Opinions 71, 74-75 (1975). (Copy enclosed.)

     4/ Letter to Hugh R. Coffin, No. A-80-08-87, copy
enclosed.

     5/ Advice to John D. Jorgenson, No. A-82-214, copy
enclosed.

Mr. L. N. Landis
January 11, 1984
Page Four


Since you have not asked whether there is a conflict as to
any specific individual or any specific decision, we have to
consider the application of the conflicts provisions of the Act
to adoption of an AQMP in the most general way. We must see
whether a conflict of interest is possible in the adoption of an
AQMP. If a conflict is possible, we cannot say, as a matter of
law, that disqualification is never required with respect to the
decision to adopt an AQMP.

In analyzing your question, it is necessary to determine
whether it is possible to answer yes to each of the following
questions

1. Does the adoption of the AQMP make it reasonably
foreseeable that any specific measure or measures will or will
not in fact be implemented by regulation by the BAAQMD?

In the materials you sent us, you included a summary table
(Table 22), which ranks the proposed stationary source control
measures in order of cost-effectiveness. The table has a
"cut-off line" between measure 23 and measure 24, showing the
staff's best estimate of which measures will have to be
implemented before 1987.

We realize that the predictions as to the results of each of
the measures in the AQMP may not be precisely accurate. As a
result, it may take implementation of somewhat more, or somewhat
fewer, measures to achieve the required air quality standards by
1987 than is indicated by the cut-off line. However, even
allowing for a margin of error in the predictions, it is
reasonably foreseeable that most of the measures above the
cut-off line will have to be implemented to meet the required
standards, and that those that are most cost-effective are most
likely to be implemented.

Therefore, assuming that the AQMP as adopted reflects the
best estimates of the BAAQMD, it is reasonably foreseeable that
the measures which are high on the list will in fact be
implemented by regulation. As a general rule, if a governmental
action -- such as the ranking of measures in the AQMP -- is
intended to accomplish a particular result, it will be assumed
that the intended result -- that the measures given highest
ranking will be implemented as proposed -- is reasonably
foreseeable. Opinion requested by Gary Gilmor, No. 76-089,
3 FPPC Opinions 38, 41 (1977).

Similarly, it is reasonably foreseeable that the measures at
the end of the list, particularly those on the contingency list,

Mr. L. N. Landis
January 11, 1984
Page Five


will not be implemented. Thus, it is reasonably foreseeable
that a BAAQMD decision to take a measure off the regular list
and to place it on the contingency list, regardless of its
estimated cost-effectiveness, will result in that measure not
being implemented. For example, the decision to place the
measure relating to lawnmowers on the contingency list, despite
its high cost-effectiveness, changed that measure's rank from
number 2 to number 30. It is reasonably foreseeable that, as a
result of that change, the measure will not be implemented.

     2. Assuming that a measure is above the cut-off line, and
thus likely to be implemented, can it be determined whether
implementation of the measure will occur in the foreseeable
future?

     The AQMP includes a year of adoption and an implementation
schedule for each measure. For example, measure number 31
(ranked #1), sets 1983 as its year of adoption, and shows
implementation beginning in 1983. Measure number 19 (ranked #2)
is scheduled for adoption in 1984, and implementation in 1985.
Therefore, by looking at the year of adoption and the
implementation schedule for a measure, it is possible to
determine whether it is likely to be implemented in the
foreseeable future.

     There is no specific time period in which a result must
occur for it to meet the foreseeability test.5/ A regulation
which is likely to be enacted and implemented within a few years
may be reasonably foreseeable, given that the regulatory process
is by its very nature time-consuming.

     3. Assuming that it is likely that a specific measure will
be implemented in the foreseeable future, can it be determined
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the implementation
will have a financial effect on a specific business entity?

     The answer to this question depends on the nature of the
measure. However, looking at the measures included in the 1982
AQMP which you sent us, it appears that many measures are
sufficiently specific to put a board member on notice that the
measure, if implemented, would affect a specific business
entity.

_____________________________

     5/ An intended result may, of course, be so remote in
time and contingent on so many uncertain intervening events,
that it is not reasonably foreseeable. Advice to Allen Robert,
A-82-04 (copy enclosed). Whether a remote event is reasonably
foreseeable depends on the specific facts of the situation.

Mr. L. N. Landis
January 11, 1984
Page Six


     For example, in measure number 1 (ranked #6), Organic
Chemical Manufacturing, the description of the control measure
states that it will control fugitive emissions from
organicchemical manufacturing plants. Thus if a BAAQMD member
had an interest in a company (such as Chevron) owning an organic
chemical manufacturing plant in the BAAQMD jurisdiction, it
would be reasonably foreseeable that the measure, when
implemented, would affect that specific company.

     4. Assuming that it is reasonably foreseeable that, as a
result of being included in the AQMD, a measure is likely to be
implemented in the foreseeable future, and to affect a
particular business entity, can it be determined whether the
measure will have a material financial effect on the specific
business entity?

     The AQMP includes several figures for each measure,
including a figure on "cost-effectiveness" per ton of the
control measure, and figures on the number of tons per day that
pollution would be reduced by the measure. Using these figures,
it is possible to arrive at a rough estimate of the financial
impact of a specific measure.

     For example, in measure number 1 (ranked #6), the estimated
cost-effectiveness is $500/ton. The potential amount of
emissions reduction is 3 tons per day. Thus the annual cost
estimate, for all companies affected on the AQMD, is $54,750.
From this figure, it appears unlikely that the effect of this
measure could be material on any large company. (Under 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18703(b)(1) an annual effect of less than
$100,000 on a large company is unlikely to be material.) On the
other hand, if the AQMP figures showed a much higher cost, such
as in measure number 2 (ranked #26, below the cut-off list)
where the total annual estimated cost is $3,285,000, it is
possible that there could be a material effect on a company
which was a major target of the regulation. When information
from the AQMP is combined with information presented at the
public hearings by a company which would be affected by the
measure, a member might well be put on notice that the effect on
the particular company would be material. In reviewing
testimony and written submissions at the hearings on the AQMP, I
observed a number of instances in which a company did make
statements about the anticipated financial effect of a measure
on that company.

     5. Assuming that is is reasonably foreseeable that a
particular measure in the AQMD will be implemented and will have
the requisite effect on a specific business entity, can it be

Mr. L. N. Landis
January 11, 1984
Page Seven


determined whether the effect will be different from the effect
on the public generally?

     Some measures in the AQMP may affect so many diverse
businesses in the Bay Area in the same way that the effect on a
specific business may be said to be indistinguishable from the
effect on the public generally.  On the other hand, some
measures clearly target a specific industry,1/  or a small,
clearly identified group of businesses.  For example, according
to the AQMP, measure number 1 (ranked #6) would affect
approximately ten chemical plants.  The effect on each of these
ten plants would clearly be distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally.

     It thus appears, after analyzing each of the necessary
elements of a conflict of interest under Section 87103, that,
under the right circumstances, it could be reasonably
foreseeable that the result of a decision to include a
particular measure in the AQMP would be that the measure would
be implemented by regulation in the foreseeable future, and that
the regulation would have a material financial effect
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on a
specific identifiable business entity.  If a BAAQMD member had
an interest in that business entity, the member would have a
conflict of interest.  Therefore we cannot say, as a matter
of law, that it is impossible for a BAAQMD member ever to have a
conflict of interest requiring disqualification in connection
with the adoption of an AQMP.

     We will be happy to advise you as to the potential for
conflict in any specific factual situation when the AQMP is next
amended.  Of course, each individual situation must be analyzed
on its own facts.

     You noted in your letter that we have advised both BAAQMD
and ABAG that, in adopting an AQMP, it may be possible to
separate out decisions on individual measures and thus to
minimize the effects of the required disqualification.  In the
event of a potential conflict as to the AQMP, you may wish to
consult us further on this subject.


_______________________________
1/  A specific industry does not constitute a significant
segment of the public generally, see 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
18703.

Mr. L. N. Wilson
January 11, 1984
Page Eight


     I trust this has clarified the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act for you.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Barbara A. Milman
                                        General Counsel

BAM:km
Copies of Oglesby, 1 FPPC 71
         Thorner, 1 FPPC 198
         A-80-87
         A-82-214
         A-82-04

BAY AREA AIR QUALITY MANAGEMENT DISTRICT

July 18, 1983

Steven R. Crooks
Legal Counsel
Enforcement Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street Building
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Crooks:

We were pleased with the results of your
investigation into possible violations of the
Conflict of Interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act as a result of votes cast by Board
Members on two specific matters. Your letter
concludes that the Fair Political Practices Commission has determined that an enforcement action
is not appropriate, and that the Commission has
closed its investigation.

However, your letter goes on to formulate
a policy on actions by the Board on adoption of
Air Quality Plans which we feel is unwarranted.

It appears unwarranted to require the Board
to follow the policy outlined in the letter when it
is acknowledged that the question "has not previously
been ruled upon by the Commission in an enforcement
proceeding" and has not even been the subject of
any Commission "opinions or advice letters on the
subject". Moreover, it appears that the letter misunderstands the nature and effect of adoption of the
AQMF. The adoption of the AQMF is really no more than
a statement of present intention that the measures
included therein will later be considered for adoption
at regularly scheduled public hearings.

Adoption of the AQMF does not constitute the adoption
of any of the specific measures included therein. In
addition, some of the elements of the AQMF relate to
"transportation control" elements which, if subsequently
adopted, will be adopted by another agency. It appears
particularly inappropriate to conclude that members of
our Board of Directors might have a conflict of interest
as a result of elements within the AQMF that can not be
subsequently adopted by the Board.

Your letter appears not to realize that this
District is one of three co-lead agencies which are
responsible for adoption of the AQMF. Certainly, any
policy which is "mandatory" for the District's Board

939 ELLIS STREET • SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94109 • (415) 771-6000

Steven R. Crooks                                  July 18, 1983
Legal Counsel, Enforcement Div.
FPPC

Page 2

relative to adoption of any revision to the AQMP would be equally applicable to the Association of Bay Area Governments and to the Metropolitan Transportation Commission (the other two agencies delegated joint responsibility for adoption of the plan).

In addition, the AQMP adopted by the District, ABAG and MTC must thereafter be approved by the ARB. Accordingly, the policy "mandated" by your letter for the District's Board of Directors would presumably also apply to the ARB as well as to the District, ABAG and MTC and every other air pollution control agency which adopts an Air Quality Plan.

Since the FPPC has not heretofore ruled on the subject, I would request that the Commission issue an official opinion on the subject, and in addition I would request that the Commission hold a public hearing on the matter so that we would have an opportunity to clear up what appears to be misconceptions concerning the nature and effect of adoption of an Air Quality Plan.

I would be pleased to discuss these matters further at your convenience.

                                          Sincerely yours,

                                          [signature]
                                          Milton Feldstein
                                          Air Pollution Control Officer

    Board of Directors, BAAQMD

The Political Reform Act requires a public official to 
disqualify himself or herself from any governmental decision in 
which the official knows or has reason to know that he or she 
nas a financial interest. 
Government Code Section 87100. 1/ 
Section 87103 provides, in part, that: 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, d~stinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally; on: 
(a) 
Any ousiness entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
more than one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(0) 
Any real property in which the pUblic 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth 
more tnan one thousand dollars ($1,000). 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans 
by a commercial lending institution in the regular 
course of business on terms available to the 
puolic without regard to official status, 
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or 
more in value provided to, received by or promised 
to the pUOlic official within 12 months prior to 
the time when the decision is made. 
Cd) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
1/ All statutory references made are to the Government 
Code unless otnerwise stated. 


Mr. L. N. Landis 
January 11, 1984 
Page Three 
A memoer of the BAAQMD Board is required to disqualify 
himself or herself from a decision on an AQMP if it is 
reasonably foreseeaole that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable- from the effect on the public 
generally, on a business entity or on real property, in which 
the official has an investment of over $1,000, from which the 
official has received income of over $250, or of which the 
official is an officer or employee. 
You should note that the key to whether an official has a 
conflict of interest is whether some specific effect of the AQMP 
decisionll on tne official's interests is "reasonably 
foreseeaole." 
The Commission has interpreted this term on 
several occasions, nolding that an effect is "reasonably 
foreseeable" whenever there is a "substantial probability" that 
it will occur. 
E.g., Opinion request by Tom Thorner, No. 
75-089, 1 FPPC Opinions 198, 204-206 (1975). 
If the effect is 
liKely, out not certain, it may still be reasonably 
foreseeaole. 
The fact that intermediate steps, SUGh as the 
adoption of a regulation to implement a policy, must" be taken, 
does not necessarily mean that an effect is not reasonably 
foreseeaole. 
For example, the Commission has specifically ruled that 
disqualification may oe required as to a governmental decision 
on the adoption of a city redevelopment pla~ or a local 
coastal plan,41 even though such plans can only be implemented 
oy future decisions, and can be amended at any time. 
Similarly, 
disqualification has been required when one city council voted 
on a proposal to recommend certain action to a second city 
council~ ~n that case, not only was the vote not on the actual 
decision, out the first city council did not even have the 
authority to implement its recommendation. 51 
Of course, the 
determination of reasonable foreseeaoility rests ultimately upon 
the specific facts of each case. 
21 
The vote on the AQMP is clearly a "governmental 
decision." 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(0) (1) 
(copy 
enclosed) • 
11 
Opinion requested by Thomas Oglesby, No. 75-083, 
1 FPPC Opinions 71, 74-75 (1975). 
(Copy enclosed.) 
41 
Letter to Hugh R. Coffin, No. A-80-08-87, copy 
enclosed. 
51 
Advice to John D. Jorgenson, No. A-82-214, copy 
enclosed. 


Mr. L. N. Landis 
January 11, 1984 
Page Four 
Since you have not asked whether there is a conflict as to 
any specific individual or any specific decision, we have to 
consider the application of the conflicts provisions of the Act 
to adoption of an AQMP in the most general way. 
We must see 
whether a conflict of interest is possible in the adoption of an 
AQMP. 
If a conflict is possible, we cannot say, as a matter of 
law, that disqualification is never required with respect to the 
decision to adopt an AQMP. 
In analyzing your question, it is necessary to determine 
whether it is possible to answer yes to each of the following
sections.question_synthetic: Can a BAAQMD Board member have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when voting on the adoption of an Air Quality Management Plan that identifies pollution control measures to be implemented by future regulations?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A BAAQMD Board member must disqualify themselves from voting on an AQMP if it is reasonably foreseeable that specific measures in the plan will be implemented through future regulations and will have a material financial effect on a business entity in which the member has a financial interest exceeding statutory thresholds. Whether disqualification is required depends on the specific facts of each case, including the foreseeability of measure implementation, the timing of implementation, and the material financial effect on specific business entities.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document dated January 11, 1984, addressing Advice Request No. A-84-09. Question section is present but conclusion section is truncated in provided text. Analysis section provides detailed guidance on conflict of interest analysis framework. Document references multiple prior FPPC opinions and regulations.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. 
Does the adoption of the AQMP make it reasonably 
foreseeable tnat any specific measure or measures will or will 
not in fact be implemented by regulation by the BAAQMD? 
In the materials you sent us, you included a summary table 
(Table 22), which ranks the proposed stationary source control 
measures in order of cost-effectiveness. 
The table has a 
"cut-off line" between measure 23 and measure 24, showing the 
staff's best estimate of wnich measures will have to be 
implemented before 1987. 
We realize that the predictions as to the results of each of 
the measures in the AQMP may not be precisely accurate. 
As a 
result, it may take implementation of somewhat more, or somewhat 
fewer, measures to achieve the required air quality standards by 
1987 than is indicated by the cut-off line. 
However, even 
allowing for a margin of error in the predictions, it is 
reasonably foreseeable that most of the measures above the 
cut-off line will have to be implemented to meet the required 
standards, and that those that are most cost-effective are most 
likely to be implemented. 
Therefore, assuming that the AQMP as adopted reflects the 
best estimates of the BAAQMD, it is reasonably foreseeable that 
tne measures which are high on the list will in fact be 
implemented by regulation. 
As a general rule, if a governmental 
action -- sucn as the ranking of measures in the AQMP -- is 
intended to accomplish a particular result, it will be assumed 
that the intended result -- that the measures given highest 
ranking will be implemented as proposed -- is reasonably 
foreseeable. - Opinion requested by Gary Gilmor, No. 76-089, 
3 FPPC Opinions 38, 41 (1977). 
Similarly, it is reasonably foreseeable that the measures at 
tne end of the list, particularly those on the contingency list, 


Mr. L. N. Landis 
January 11, 1984 
Page Five 
will not De implemented. 
Thus, it is reasonably foreseeable 
that a BAAQMD decision to take a measure off the regular list 
and to place it on the contingency list, regardless of its 
estimated cost-effectiveness, will result in that measure not 
being implemented. 
For example, the decision to place the 
measure relating to lawnmowers on the contingency list, despite 
its high cost-effectiveness, changed that measure's rank from 
number 2 to number 30. 
It is reasonably foreseeable that, as a 
result of that change, the measure will not be implemented. 
2. 
Assuming that a measure is above the cut-off line, and 
thus likely to oe implemented, can it be determined whether 
implementation of the measure will occur in the foreseeable 
future? 
The AQMP includes a year of adoption and an implementation 
schedule for each measure. 
For example, measure number 31 
(ranked #1), sets 1983 as its year of adoption, and shows 
implementation Degining in 1983. 
Measure number 19 (ranked #2) 
is scneduled for adoption in 1984, and implementation in 1985. 
Therefore, by looking at the year of adoption and the 
implementation schedule for a measure, it is possible to 
determine whether it is likely to be implemented in the 
foreseeaDle future. 
There is no specific time period in which a result must 
occur for it to meet the foreseeaDility test. 6/ 
A regulation 
which is likely to be enacted and implemented within a few years 
may De reasonably foreseeable, given that the regulatory process 
is by its very nature time-consuming. 
3. 
Assuming that it is likely that a specific measure will 
De implemented in the foreseeable future, can it be determined 
whether it is reasonaoly foreseeable that the implementation 
will have a financial effect on a specific business entity? 
The answer to this question depends on the nature of the 
measure. 
However, looking at the measures included in the 1982 
AQMP which you sent us, it appears that many measures are 
suffiCiently specific to put a board member on notice that the 
measure, if implemented, would affect a specific business 
entity. 
~/ An intended result may, of course, De so remote in 
time and contingent on so many uncertain intervening events, 
that it is not reasonably foreseeable. 
Advice to Allen Robert, 
A-82-04 (copy enclosed). 
Whether a remote event is reasonaDly 
foreseeable depends on the specific facts of the situation. 


Mr. L. N. Landis 
January 11, 1984 
Page Six 
For example, in measure number 1 (ranked 16), Organic 
Chemical Manufacturing, the description of the control measure 
states that it will control fugitive omissions from 
organicchemical manufacturing plants. 
Thus if a BAAQMD member 
had an interest in a company (such as Chevron) owning an organic 
chemical manufacturing plant in the BAAQMD jurisdiction, it 
would be reasonably foreseeable that the measure, when 
implemented, would affect that specific company. 
4. 
Assuming that it is reasonably foreseeable that, as a 
result of being included in the AQMD, a measure is likely to be 
implemented in the foreseeable future, and to affect a 
particular business entity, can it be determined whether the 
measure will have a material financial effect on the specific 
business entity? 
The AQMP includes several figures for each measure, 
including a figure on ·cost-effectiveness· per ton of the 
control measure, and figures on the number of tons per day that 
pollution would be reduced by the measure. 
Using these figures, 
it is possible to arrive at a rough estimate of the financial 
impact of a specific measure. 
For example, in measure number 1 (ranked 16), the estimated 
cost-effectiveness is $500jton. 
The potential amount of 
emissions reduction is 3 tons per day. 
Thus the annual cost 
estimate, for all companies affected on the AQMP, is $54,750. 
From this figure, it appears unlikely that the effect of this 
measure could be material on any large company. 
(Under 2 Cal. 
Adm. Code Section 18703(b) (1) an annual effect of less than 
$100,000 on a large company is unlikely to be material.) 
On the 
other hand, if the AQMP figures showed a much higher cost, such 
as in measure number 2 (ranked 126, below the cut-off line) 
where the total annual estimated cost is $3,285,000, it is 
possible that there could be a material effect on a company 
which was a major target of the regulation. 
When information 
from the AQMP is combined with information presented at the 
public hearings by a company which would be affected by the 
measure, a member might well be put on notice that the effect on 
the particular company would be material. 
In reviewing 
testimony and written submissions at the hearings on the AQMP, I 
observed a number of instances in which a company did make 
statements about the anticipated financial effect of a measure 
on that company. 
5. 
Assuming that is is reasonably foreseeable that a 
particular measure in the AQMD will be implemented and will have 
the requisite effect on a specific business entity, can it be 


Mr. L. N. Landis 
January 11, 1984 
Page Seven 
determined whether the effect will be different from the effect 
on the public generally? 
Some measures in the AQMP may affect so many diverse 
businesses in the Bay Area in the same way that the effect on a 
specific business may De said to De indistinguishable from the 
effect on the public generally. 
On the other hand, some 
measures clearly target a specific industry,lI or a small, 
clearly identified group of businesses. 
For example, according 
to the AQMP, measure number 1 (ranked #6) would affect 
approximately ten chemical plants. 
The effect on each of these 
ten plants would clearly be distinguishable from the effect on 
the pUblic generally. 
It thus appears, after analyzing eacn of the necessary 
elements of a conflict of interest under Section 87103, that, 
under the rignt circumstances, it could be reasonably 
foreseeable that tne result of a decision to include a 
particular'measure in the AQMP would be that tne measure would 
be implemented by regulation in the foreseeable future, and that 
the regulation would nave a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect on the'public generally, on a 
specific identifiable business entity. If a BAAQMD member nad 
an interest in that business entity, the member would have a 
conflict of interest. Therefore we cannot say, as a matter 
of law, that it is impossible for a BAAQMD member ever to have a 
conflict of interest requiring disqualification in connection 
with the adoption of an AQMP. 
We will be happy to advise you as to the potential for 
conflict in any specific factual situation when the AQMP is next 
amended. 
Of course, each individual situation must be analyzed 
on its own facts. 
You noted in your letter that we have advised both BAAQMD 
and the ABAG that, in adopting an AQMP, it may be possible to 
separate out decisions on individual measures and thus to 
minimize the effects of tne required disqualification. 
In the 
event of a potential conflict as to the AQMP, you may wish to 
consult us further on this suoject. 
ZI 
A specific industry does not constitute a significant 
segment of the public generally, see 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18703. 


Mr. L. N. Wilson 
January 11, 1984 
Page Eight 
I trust this has clarified the conflict of interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act for you. 
BAM:km 
Copies of Oglesby, 1 FPPC 71 
Tnorner, 1 FPPC 198 
A-8 0-8 7 
A-82-214 
A-82-04

CONCLUSION: A BAAQMD Board member must disqualify themselves from voting on an AQMP if it is reasonably foreseeable that specific measures in the plan will be implemented through future regulations and will have a material financial effect on a business entity in which the member has a financial interest exceeding statutory thresholds. Whether disqualification is required depends on the specific facts of each case, including the foreseeability of measure implementation, the timing of implementation, and the material financial effect on specific business entities.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 January 11, 1984 L. N. "Judge" Landis Chairperson Bay Area Air Quality Management District 939 Ellis Street San Francisco, CA 94109 S3-79 Re: Advice Request No. A-84-005 Dear Mr. Landis: You have asked whether a member of the Bay Area Air Quality Management District ("BAAQMD") Board can have a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act with respect to the adoption of an Air Quality Management Plan ("AQMP"). You have enclosed a general description of an AQMP, as well the actual AQMP adopted by BAAQMD in 1982. The Clean Air Act amendments adopted by Congress in 1977 require states to develop plans to meet statutory air quality standards by 1987. As part of this process, an AQMP must be adopted for the Bay Area. The AQMP is developed and approved, after public hearings, by BAAQMD, the Metropolitan Transportation Commission and the Association of Bay Area Governments. The AQMP identifies measures to be adopted in order to achieve the required air quality standards. The measures describe sources of pollution, the types of emission, and the means of reducing pollution, to be addressed by BAAQMD regulations. The measures on the list for adoption are ranked by the staff in order of their cost-effectiveness. Measures not approved by the BAAQMD for adoption are put on contingency status and are placed at the end of the list. A cut-off line is drawn at that point on the list at which, if the pollution reduction predictions for the measures are accurate, the required air quality standards would be met. If the predictions are accurate, it would be necessary to implement all the measures above the cut-off line in order to meet the Federal requirements by 1987; it would not be necessary to implement the Mr. L. N. Landis January 11, 1984 Page Two measures below the line. A measure is not effective, however, until BAAQMD adopts a specific regulation to implement the measure. Thus, the AQMP does not directly regulate any company or industry. It merely identifies the measures for which regulations will be adopted. ANALYSIS The Political Reform Act requires a public official to disqualify himself or herself from any governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to know that he or she has a financial interest. Government Code Section 87100.1/ Section 87103 provides, in part, that: An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: (a) Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000). (b) Any real property in which the public
embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that BAAQMD Board members may be required to disqualify themselves from voting on Air Quality Management Plans if measures in the plan are reasonably foreseeable to be implemented through future regulations and would have material financial effects on businesses in which the member has reportable financial interests. The determination requires analysis of whether specific measures will likely be implemented, when implementation will occur, and whether implementation will materially affect specific business entities.
